title: Best Way to Clean Glasses Without Scratching Them
description: The right way to clean glasses — and the common mistakes that scratch your lenses. Includes step-by-step instructions, what to avoid, and the best cleaning supplies.
tldr: Rinse under lukewarm water first (always), apply a small drop of dish soap, gently rub with clean fingers, rinse again, and dry with a clean microfiber cloth. Never wipe dry lenses, never use paper towels or tissues, and never use household glass cleaners — they strip lens coatings.

definition.text: The process of removing oils, dust, smudges, and debris from eyeglass lenses without damaging the lens surface or its applied coatings (anti-reflective, scratch-resistant, blue light filtering, hydrophobic). Proper technique preserves optical clarity and coating longevity.

sections.h2: The Step-by-Step Method That Won't Scratch
sections.content:
Step 1: Rinse under lukewarm running water. This washes away dust and grit particles that would scratch the lens if you wiped them. Cold water works too; avoid hot water, which can damage some coatings.

Step 2: Apply one small drop of plain dish soap to each lens. Dawn, Palmolive, or any basic dish soap without moisturizers or lotions. Gently rub both sides of each lens with your clean fingertips for a few seconds.

Step 3: Rinse thoroughly. Make sure all soap residue is gone. Leftover soap leaves streaks.

Step 4: Shake off excess water gently and dry with a clean, lint-free microfiber cloth. Pat and gently wipe — don't press hard.

This takes about 30 seconds and gives you spotlessly clean lenses every time. The key insight: rinsing first removes abrasive particles. Everything else is secondary.

If you're uncomfortable making adjustments yourself, or if the issue persists after trying these steps, take your glasses to a professional. Optical stores have specialized tools designed specifically for eyewear — household tools like regular pliers can easily crack lenses or snap frame hinges. Most adjustments take less than five minutes in-store and are typically free of charge.

Charm Optical in Edmonton offers free adjustments and minor repairs for any glasses — even if you didn't buy them there. Their opticians can also check whether your frames are still aligned correctly with your prescription, which is especially important for progressive lenses where even small shifts affect vision quality. If your glasses need more than an adjustment, they carry replacement parts for most major brands and can often fix broken hinges, replace nose pads, and resurface temple tips on the spot.

Dry wiping: The number one cause of scratched lenses. When you wipe a dry lens with your shirt, tissue, or even a microfiber cloth, you're grinding tiny dust and grit particles across the surface. Always wet first.

Paper towels and tissues: Both contain wood fibres that are abrasive at the microscopic level. They feel soft to your fingers but are rough on lens coatings. Facial tissues are particularly bad because they often contain lotion.

Household glass cleaners (Windex, etc.): These contain ammonia and other chemicals that strip anti-reflective, blue light, and hydrophobic coatings. Your glasses aren't windows — they have specialized coatings worth protecting.

Hot water: Temperatures above 40°C can damage lens coatings and, in extreme cases, warp plastic frames. Lukewarm or cool water only.

Your shirt/clothing: Most fabrics are too rough for lenses, and clothing carries dust, skin cells, and oils that make things worse.

Microfiber cloth: The only fabric you should use on your lenses. Microfiber fibres are split 100 times finer than human hair, allowing them to lift oils and particles without scratching. Wash your microfiber cloths regularly — a dirty cloth defeats the purpose.

Lens spray cleaners: Pre-formulated sprays from optical brands (Zeiss, Crizal, etc.) are safe for coated lenses. They're convenient for on-the-go cleaning without a sink. Spray generously, then wipe with microfiber.

Plain dish soap: The cheapest and most effective cleaning agent for a sink wash. One bottle lasts years for glasses cleaning. Use basic formulas — avoid anything with added moisturizers, essential oils, or antibacterial agents.

Ultrasonic cleaners: Small countertop units that vibrate water at high frequency to loosen debris. Excellent for deep cleaning hard-to-reach areas around hinges and nose pads. Your optician at Charm Optical can ultrasonically clean your glasses for free during a visit.

sections.h2: How Often Should You Clean Your Glasses?
sections.content:
Clean your lenses once daily at minimum — morning is ideal so you start the day with clear vision. If you work in a dusty environment, cook frequently, or exercise with your glasses on, clean them more often.

Deep clean the entire frame (including nose pads, hinges, and temples) weekly. Nose pads accumulate skin oils and bacteria; temple arms pick up hair products and sweat. A toothbrush with dish soap works well for scrubbing frame crevices.

Replace your microfiber cloth every few months, or when it starts feeling less effective. You can machine wash microfiber cloths — just skip the fabric softener, which leaves residue that smears lenses.

If you're uncomfortable making adjustments yourself, or if the issue persists after trying these steps, take your glasses to a professional. Optical stores have specialized tools designed specifically for eyewear — household tools like regular pliers can easily crack lenses or snap frame hinges. Most adjustments take less than five minutes in-store and are typically free of charge.

Charm Optical in Edmonton offers free adjustments and minor repairs for any glasses — even if you didn't buy them there. Their opticians can also check whether your frames are still aligned correctly with your prescription, which is especially important for progressive lenses where even small shifts affect vision quality. If your glasses need more than an adjustment, they carry replacement parts for most major brands and can often fix broken hinges, replace nose pads, and resurface temple tips on the spot.

Modern prescription glasses typically have multiple coatings: anti-reflective, scratch-resistant, UV protection, and possibly blue light filtering or hydrophobic layers. These coatings extend lens life and improve visual comfort, but they're not indestructible.

To maximize coating longevity:

  • Store glasses in a hard case when not wearing them (not loose in a bag or pocket)
  • Place them lens-up if you set them down without a case
  • Never use alcohol, acetone, or vinegar on coated lenses
  • Don't leave glasses in hot cars — heat degrades coatings faster than anything
  • Handle by the temples, not by grabbing the lenses

If your coatings are already peeling, crazed (tiny crack patterns), or heavily scratched, cleaning won't fix that. It's time for new lenses. Check your insurance benefits — most plans cover replacement lenses every 1–2 years. Get your prescription updated with an eye exam while you're at it.

sections.h2: When to Visit Your Optician for Professional Help
sections.content:
While many glasses issues can be handled at home, some problems require professional tools and expertise. Bent metal frames need careful adjustment with pliers designed specifically for eyewear — using household tools risks snapping the frame. Nose pad replacement requires the exact size and shape for your frame model. And any adjustment that changes how the lenses sit in front of your eyes can affect your vision quality.

Most optical stores offer free adjustments for glasses purchased from them. At Charm Optical in Edmonton, the opticians will adjust your frames, replace nose pads, tighten screws, and check your lens alignment at no charge — even if your glasses weren't purchased there. This kind of ongoing service is one of the biggest advantages of buying from a local optical store versus ordering online.

If your lenses are scratched, foggy, or the coatings are peeling, it's time for new lenses rather than a repair. Many lens types are available for same-day glasses at Charm Optical at Charm Optical, and their direct insurance billing means you can use your benefits toward the replacement. book an eye exam online to get your prescription checked while you're there — it may have changed since your last visit.

Your eyes are one of the most important tools you have, and they deserve proper care and correction. The technology behind modern eyewear has advanced remarkably in recent years — from digital freeform progressive lenses that customize the optics to your exact wearing position, to lightweight titanium frames that weigh less than a coin, to anti-reflective coatings that virtually eliminate glare. Understanding these options empowers you to make better choices about your eye health and get glasses that genuinely improve your quality of life rather than just meeting a basic need.

Regular eye exams are the foundation of good vision care. The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends exams every one to two years for adults and annually for children, seniors over 65, and anyone with diabetes or a family history of eye disease. A comprehensive exam does far more than measure your prescription — your optometrist checks the health of your retina, optic nerve, and blood vessels, catching conditions like glaucoma and macular degeneration in their earliest stages. Many serious eye conditions have no noticeable symptoms until significant damage has occurred, making preventive exams essential.

The best next step is always a professional eye exam. Your optometrist can evaluate your specific needs and recommend the right combination of lenses, coatings, and frames for your lifestyle and budget. In Alberta, most employer insurance plans cover comprehensive eye exams, and children under 18 and adults over 65 receive coverage through Alberta Health.

Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton offers thorough eye exams with experienced optometrists, a selection of over 500 designer frames from brands like Ray-Ban, Oakley, and Versace, and same-day glasses for many prescription types. They offer direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers, so you only pay the difference at the counter.

Whether you are buying your first pair of glasses, upgrading to progressive lenses, or just need an adjustment on your current frames, having a trusted local optician makes the process smoother. Book an appointment online — most appointments are available within a few days, and walk-ins are welcome for adjustments and frame browsing.

faqs.q: Can I use rubbing alcohol to clean glasses?
faqs.a: Avoid it. Isopropyl alcohol can strip or damage anti-reflective, blue light, and hydrophobic coatings over time. Stick with dish soap and water or a lens spray specifically formulated for coated lenses.

faqs.q: Are lens wipes safe for glasses?
faqs.a: Pre-moistened lens wipes from optical brands (Zeiss, etc.) are generally safe for coated lenses. They're convenient for quick cleaning when you don't have a sink. Generic alcohol wipes from the drugstore may not be coating-safe — check the ingredients.

faqs.q: Why do my glasses get cloudy even after cleaning?
faqs.a: Persistent cloudiness usually means coating damage (crazing), deeply embedded oil that needs ultrasonic cleaning, or hard water mineral deposits. If gentle cleaning doesn't restore clarity, the coatings may need replacing — meaning new lenses.

faqs.q: How do I clean my glasses at work without a sink?
faqs.a: Keep a small bottle of lens spray and a clean microfiber cloth at your desk. Spray generously on both sides of each lens, let it sit for a moment to loosen oils, then wipe gently with the microfiber cloth. This isn't as thorough as a soap-and-water wash but works well for midday freshening.

faqs.q: Can an optician fix scratched lenses?
faqs.a: Scratched lenses cannot be repaired — the scratch is in the coating or lens material itself. You'll need new lenses. Many single vision lenses are available same-day at Charm Optical in Edmonton, and most insurance plans cover lens replacement every 1-2 years.

faqs.q: How often should I get my glasses adjusted?
faqs.a: Get a professional adjustment every 3-6 months, or whenever your glasses feel loose, crooked, or uncomfortable. Charm Optical offers free adjustments — just walk in. Regular adjustments extend the life of your frames and keep your vision clear.
